result:
# The Curious Art of Getting Lost

There's something deeply unsettling about not knowing where you are. The moment when familiar landmarks disappear, when the mental map in your head stops corresponding to the reality around you, when the comforting weight of certainty lifts away like morning fog. Most of us spend considerable energy avoiding this feeling, armed with smartphones that track our every step, guided by the patient voices of navigation apps that recalculate our routes without judgment when we miss a turn. But I've been thinking lately about what we lose when we never get lost.

I remember the last time I was truly, magnificently lost. It was in Venice, which is perhaps the world's most perfect city for losing oneself. The narrow alleys twist and terminate without warning, bridges appear where you don't expect them, and the same church seems to materialize on your left, then your right, then somehow behind you. My phone had died, the paper map I'd picked up from the hotel was somewhere at the bottom of the canal (a victim of an enthusiastic gesture while talking), and the sun was setting, painting the crumbling facades in shades of amber and rose that made everything look both more beautiful and more alien.

At first, I felt that familiar flutter of panic. The scheduled dinner reservation, the friend waiting at the hotel, the basic human need to know one's place in space—all of these pressed against my chest like a weight. But then something shifted. Maybe it was the way the light caught on the water, or the sound of someone practicing violin in an upper window, or simply the exhaustion of anxiety giving way to acceptance. I stopped trying to find my way and started simply being where I was.

This is harder than it sounds. We're constantly oriented toward the future, toward the next thing, the destination, the goal. Even our leisure is structured this way—we go on vacation to see specific sights, to check boxes, to accumulate experiences like stamps in a passport. But being lost forces you into the present moment with an intensity that's hard to achieve any other way. Every corner becomes a decision, every street a possibility, every doorway a potential discovery.

That evening in Venice, I found a tiny osteria that I would never have discovered otherwise, tucked into a courtyard that seemed to exist in its own pocket of time. The owner, seeing my obvious foreign confusion, took pity on me and not only fed me the most remarkable seafood risotto I've ever tasted but also drew me a map on a napkin—not to my hotel, but to three other places he insisted I needed to see before leaving Venice. "These are for tomorrow," he said in careful English. "Tonight, you are here."

There's a word in Japanese—'tsundoku'—which means acquiring books and letting them pile up unread. I think we need a similar word for the places we mean to go but never visit, the streets we pass but never turn down, the doors we notice but never open. When we're efficiently navigating from point A to point B, we accumulate these missed opportunities like debt. Being lost is like declaring bankruptcy on that debt, starting fresh with no obligations except to the present moment.

The philosopher Walter Benjamin wrote about the flâneur, the urban wanderer who treats city streets like a living room, who makes an art of aimless strolling. But the flâneur chooses to wander; being lost is wandering without choice, which creates a different kind of attention. The lost person looks at everything as a potential clue, a possible landmark, a breadcrumb on the trail back to the known world. This looking is intense and careful in a way that casual observation rarely is.

I think about how children experience space before they develop what psychologists call cognitive mapping—the ability to create mental representations of physical spaces. For very young children, every journey is an adventure because they haven't yet built these internal maps. The way to grandmother's house is not a series of streets and turns but a story: past the house with the scary dog, around the corner where the ice cream truck parks, through the tunnel of trees that makes everything green. They're constantly lost and constantly finding, and the world maintains its capacity to surprise.

As adults, we've mapped everything, or at least we believe we have. We know the fastest route to work, the best coffee shop in the neighborhood, the shortcuts that save five minutes on the morning commute. This knowledge is useful, even essential, but it also creates a kind of blindness. When you know where you're going, you stop seeing where you are.

I once met a photographer who had a practice of getting deliberately lost once a month. He would take a train to a random stop, or have someone drive him to an unknown neighborhood with his eyes closed, or simply walk until nothing looked familiar. Then he would spend the day finding his way back, but slowly, with his camera, documenting not just what he saw but how it felt to see it without context. His photos from these expeditions had a quality I've rarely seen in urban photography—a sense of genuine discovery, as if he were the first person to notice the way light fell on that particular wall, or how those particular windows aligned with that particular slice of sky.

This practice of intentional lostness seems both luxurious and necessary. Luxurious because it requires time we claim not to have, necessary because without it we risk living in an ever-shrinking world. The paradox of modern navigation technology is that while it has made the entire globe theoretically accessible, it has also made our actual lived geography smaller. We move through tubes of certainty, from known point to known point, rarely venturing into the spaces between.

But the spaces between are where life happens. They're where you might strike up a conversation with a stranger because you need directions, where you might notice a used bookstore you've passed a hundred times without seeing, where you might discover that the city you thought you knew has entire neighborhoods you've never explored, communities you've never encountered, stories you've never heard.

There's also something to be said for the humility of being lost. In a culture that prizes competence and control, not knowing where you are is a small defeat, a tiny crack in the armor of adulthood. But these cracks are where the light gets in, to paraphrase Leonard Cohen. When you're lost, you have to ask for help. You have to admit ignorance. You have to trust strangers. These are not comfortable experiences for most of us, but they're humanizing ones.

I think about the great explorers, the ones we celebrate in history books, and how most of their time was spent being lost. Magellan didn't know where he was going; he was looking for a passage that might not exist. Columbus thought he was somewhere else entirely. Even with their crude maps and astronomical instruments, they were essentially sailing into the unknown, making it up as they went along. We frame their stories as tales of discovery, but they're really stories about being productively lost.

Of course, there's a privilege in choosing to be lost, in having the safety net of knowing you can become found again whenever you choose. Real lostness—the kind that comes with displacement, exile, or emergency—is not romantic. It's terrifying. The refugee who cannot return home, the person with dementia who cannot find their way back to themselves, the child separated from their parents in a crowd—these are not adventures but tragedies. The ability to be playfully lost depends on the security of having somewhere to return to.

But for those of us fortunate enough to have that security, avoiding all experiences of disorientation seems like a missed opportunity. We spend so much time trying to find ourselves—through therapy, meditation, self-help books, personality tests—but maybe sometimes we need to lose ourselves first. Maybe the self we're trying to find is actually constructed from all the moments when we didn't know where we were, when we had to pay attention, when we had to choose a direction without knowing where it led.

There's a scene in almost every hero's journey where the protagonist enters an unmapped forest, a place where there are no paths because no one has been there before. This is both a metaphor and a practical necessity of growth. You can't discover anything new by following existing paths. You have to get lost first.

I've been trying to practice getting lost in small ways. Taking different routes home. Turning off the GPS and trying to navigate by instinct. Saying yes to invitations to neighborhoods I don't know. It's harder than it should be—the pull of efficiency is strong, and there's always something that seems more important than wandering. But each time I manage it, I'm reminded of how much there is to see when you're not looking for anything in particular.

Last week, I got off the bus three stops early just to walk through an unfamiliar area. I found a community garden I didn't know existed, hidden behind a row of houses, tended by elderly residents who've probably been growing vegetables there for decades. One of them, seeing me peering through the fence, invited me in and spent twenty minutes showing me his tomato plants, explaining how his grandmother taught him to pinch off the suckers, how the soil in this particular spot was special because it used to be a riverbed. I would never have had that conversation if I'd stayed on the bus, if I'd remained on my efficient path from here to there.

The thing about being lost is that it makes you available to the world in a way that knowing where you're going doesn't. When you have a destination, you're arrow-shaped, pointed toward a future moment. When you're lost, you're more like a sphere, equally open to all directions. This openness can be uncomfortable—it's why we reach for our phones the moment we feel uncertain about our location. But it can also be liberating.

I think about the Venice evening often, not because I eventually found my way back (though I did, with the help of a kind water taxi driver who took pity on my terrible Italian), but because of how it felt to surrender to lostness. For those few hours, I existed in a parallel Venice, one that belonged to no guidebook, that appeared and disappeared with each turn, that was mine alone even as it was utterly beyond my control.

We live in an age of unprecedented documentation and mapping. Satellite images have captured every inch of the earth's surface. Street View cameras have photographed millions of miles of roads. We can explore distant cities from our couches, read reviews of restaurants we'll never visit, trace the routes of journeys we'll never take. But all this knowing, all this mapping, all this documentation—it might be making the world smaller rather than larger, more predictable rather than more wondrous.

The poet Rainer Maria Rilke wrote, "Perhaps all the dragons in our lives are princesses who are only waiting to see us act, just once, with beauty and courage. Perhaps everything that frightens us is, in its deepest essence, something helpless that wants our love." I think being lost is one of these dragons. We fear it because it makes us feel helpless, but maybe that helplessness is not a weakness but an opportunity—to see with fresh eyes, to ask for help, to discover that the world is bigger and stranger and more generous than we thought.

As I write this, I'm sitting in a coffee shop I've been to a hundred times, on a street I know well, in a neighborhood where I've lived for years. Everything is familiar, mapped, known. But if I pay attention, if I look with the eyes of someone who is lost, I notice things: the way the barista has a different smile for each regular customer, the pattern the afternoon light makes on the floor, the conversation in a language I don't recognize at the next table. Even here, in this most familiar of places, there's the possibility of being lost, of seeing as if for the first time, of discovering something new in the known world.

Maybe that's the real art of being lost—not waiting for those rare moments when we don't know where we are, but cultivating the ability to become lost wherever we find ourselves, to maintain what Zen Buddhists call "beginner's mind," to resist the dulling effects of familiarity. Maybe we can be lost on our own street, in our own home, in our own skin, and maybe that lostness is not a problem to be solved but a state to be explored.

The sun is setting now, and I need to head home. I know the way—turn left at the corner, straight for three blocks, right at the light. But maybe today I'll turn right at the corner instead, just to see what happens, just to get a little bit lost on the way to being found.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
